explanation: This requirement states that whenever prop_1 is true, then prop_0 must also be true at the same time. This is a safety property that can be encoded in LTL as globally (always), if prop_1 holds, then prop_0 holds as well.

The phrase 'The prop_0 when the prop_1 (not standby)' suggests that prop_0 should hold whenever prop_1 holds. In other words, at every moment where prop_1 is true, prop_0 must also be true. This can be captured as 'globally (if prop_1 then prop_0)', i.e., whenever prop_1 is true, prop_0 is required to be true as well.

This requirement says that when 'prop_1 and prop_2' hold, the autopilot must satisfy 'prop_0'. In LTL, to state that whenever the condition (prop_1 & prop_2) holds, then 'prop_0' must also hold at that time, we use the global operator G to enforce this throughout all time steps.

The statement is ambiguous, but assuming 'The prop_0 when the prop_1 (prop_2)' means that prop_0 holds whenever prop_1 and prop_2 both hold, this translates to: Globally, if both prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 must also be true.

The formula expresses that whenever prop_1 holds, then prop_0 holds at the same time, under the condition that prop_2 holds as additional context. Assuming that 'when the prop_1 (prop_2)' indicates when both prop_1 and prop_2 hold, prop_0 should also hold. Thus, globally, whenever both prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 must be true as well.

The task is to express that when 'prop_1' becomes true, some state change should occur (from STANDBY to TRANSITION), but we are restricted to only the atomic propositions ['prop_0', 'prop_1']. Since we can't name or refer directly to 'STANDBY' or 'TRANSITION' as atomic propositions, and no mapping between these states and the atomic propositions is provided, it is impossible to encode the specific state transition only using 'prop_1' and 'prop_0'.

The requirement states that whenever both prop_1 and prop_2 occur together, the autopilot shall ensure prop_0 holds. This can be translated to: globally, if prop_1 and prop_2 both hold, then prop_0 must also hold at that moment.

The requirement says that the autopilot should do "prop_0" whenever "prop_1" is true, and specifically when it is not in standby. That is, whenever prop_1 is true, prop_0 should also be true at that moment. In LTL, this is globally (always): if prop_1 then prop_0.

The phrase 'The prop_0 when the prop_1 (not standby)' suggests that whenever prop_1 holds, prop_0 should also hold at the same time point. In LTL, this is often captured using 'globally (prop_1 -> prop_0)', meaning at all times, if prop_1 is true, then prop_0 is also true.

This requirement says that whenever 'prop_1' is true (and not in 'standby', but standby is not an atomic proposition so we consider just 'prop_1'), then 'prop_0' must also hold. So, globally, if 'prop_1' holds, then 'prop_0' must hold.
